_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
**1. BEAM Portal Account and Filing Infrastructure (Gap: must be built before each May 1 filing)**
The workspace must establish and maintain an operational BEAM portal account for each covered NYC facility. BEAM (Building Energy Analysis Manager) is the mandatory online platform for LL97 compliance report submission, tracking, and DOB communication. Filing is a multi-step, multi-day process: DOB NOW fee payment must precede ESPM data sharing, which must precede BEAM submission, with overnight data transfer delays between each step. Three email addresses (owner, owner's representative, energy service provider) must be registered consistently across DOB NOW and BEAM. The workspace should designate a compliance lead with BEAM system access for each covered building BIN. Scope: all covered NYC facilities. Deadline: operational before each annual May 1 filing window; for 2026, functional before April 2026.

**2. ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager (ESPM) Integration (Gap: likely partial — LL84 benchmarking may already be in ESPM, but LL97 data sharing and property-type mapping must be validated)**
LL97 report data flows from ESPM to BEAM. The workspace must ensure that each covered building's ESPM account is (a) active and current with 12 months of utility data by fuel type, (b) mapped to the correct ESPM property type from the 60 LL97-recognized categories (e.g., Storage, Office, etc.), and (c) authorized to share data with the BEAM portal. Note: ESPM's native emissions calculations use different coefficients than LL97 requires — building owners must not rely on ESPM's default output; BEAM applies LL97-specific carbon coefficients. Scope: all covered buildings. Deadline: data current before each April filing preparation window.

**3. Registered Design Professional (RDP) Engagement (Gap: external vendor relationship required by law)**
The law mandates that each annual compliance report be certified by a licensed Professional Engineer or Registered Architect. The workspace must contract with a qualified RDP for each covered facility. For any building participating in the Good Faith Effort (GFE) pathway, the RDP must also certify the decarbonization plan and verify completed upgrade work. Scope: all covered buildings under Article 320. Deadline: RDP engagement before each filing cycle; for extension eligibility in prior years, RDP had to be retained by February 1.

**4. Energy Metering and Monitoring Systems (Gap: likely significant for warehouse/storage facilities)**
LL97 compliance depends on accurate fuel-type energy consumption data. Warehouses and logistics facilities classified as Storage Group S face particular challenges from high-bay lighting, dock door operations, and 24/7 climate control systems. The workspace should implement circuit-level sub-metering across all covered facilities to provide real-time energy consumption visibility by system and floor, enabling accurate emissions calculation and identification of reduction opportunities. LL88 (companion law) independently requires electrical sub-meters in covered tenant spaces greater than 5,000 sq ft, with attestation via BEAM. Scope: all covered NYC facilities, priority on storage/warehouse verticals. Deadline: LL88 sub-metering obligations already in force; LL97 monitoring as ongoing operational requirement.

**5. Lease Clause Audit and Green Lease Clause Insertion (Gap: exposure to landlord cost pass-through on leased facilities)**
For leased NYC facilities, the workspace must audit all existing commercial lease agreements for provisions allowing landlords to pass LL97 compliance costs (retrofit capital, penalty costs) through to tenants on a prorated basis. Future leases should include negotiated caps or clarifications on LL97 cost pass-through. For owned or ground-leased facilities, the workspace should consider inserting green lease provisions into any tenant sub-leases to enable recovery of compliance costs from tenants responsible for significant energy consumption. Scope: all NYC commercial leases. Deadline: audit existing leases within 60 days; incorporate into all new lease negotiations.

**6. 2030 Capital Planning and Retrofit Program (Gap: ~57% of covered buildings projected non-compliant without action)**
The 2030–2034 compliance period imposes approximately 50% deeper emissions cuts than 2024–2029. For Storage Group S facilities the intensity limit drops from 0.00426 to approximately 0.00111 tCO₂e/sq ft/yr — a roughly 74% reduction. The workspace must initiate a capital planning program covering: HVAC electrification (commercial heat pumps replacing gas/oil boilers); LED lighting with occupancy controls; building envelope improvements (insulation, air sealing); and Building Management Systems (BMS). The Beneficial Electrification Credit (introduced April 2024) provides compliance credit for early installation of high-efficiency heating, cooling, and domestic hot water systems before 2030, with higher credits for installations before 2026. The workspace should assess REC procurement as a bridge measure (RECs may offset up to 10% of emissions in 2024–2029, reduced to 5% in 2030+). Scope: all covered NYC facilities with projected 2030 overage. Deadline: capital plan by Q4 2026; procurement and construction 2027–2029.

*Source: NYC DOB LL97 Compliance Report Submission Process PDF. https://www.nyc.gov/assets/buildings/pdf/ll97-compliance-report-process.pdf. NYC Accelerator LL97. https://accelerator.nyc/ll97. Envigilance LL97 Warehouse Guide. https://envigilance.com/energy-monitoring/nyc-building-energy-laws/local-law-97/ll97-warehouse/. Urban Green Council LL97 page. https://www.urbangreencouncil.org/what-we-do/driving-innovative-policy/ll97/.*
